era_context: Late 14th century: The papacy returns to Rome after the Avignon period. Edward III of England dies, ending an era. The Ming Dynasty consolidates power in China. The Ottoman Empire expands in the Balkans. The Hundred Years' War continues. African and Asian civilizations develop independently of European awareness.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; oral traditions not systematically documented)
Southeast Asia (sparse European sources; local chronicles underrepresented)
Americas (no European contact; indigenous records limited)

events.title: Edward III dies; Richard II becomes King of England
events.description: Edward III, King of England and initiator of the Hundred Years' War, died on 21 June 1377 at age 64. He was succeeded by his grandson Richard II, who was only 10 years old. Edward III's death marked the end of an era of English military dominance and the beginning of a period of internal political instability. Richard's minority necessitated a regency, and the young king's later reign would be marked by conflict with Parliament, the Peasants' Revolt (1381), and ultimately his deposition in 1399. Edward III's death also had implications for the Hundred Years' War, as the aging king had been the driving force behind English claims to the French throne.
key_figures: Richard II (King of England, r. 1377–1399, age 10 at accession)
John of Gaunt, Duke of Lancaster (regent)

sources.name: English royal records and parliamentary rolls

events.certainty_note: Edward III's death date (21 June 1377) is well-documented in English royal records. Richard II's accession and age are clearly attested.

events.title: Gregory XI dies; Urban VI elected Pope
events.description: Pope Gregory XI died on 27 March 1377. He had been the last pope to reside in Avignon before returning the papacy to Rome in 1377, ending the Avignon Papacy (1309–1377). Gregory XI's death created a succession crisis. The Roman populace and cardinals demanded an Italian pope to keep the papacy in Rome. On 8 April 1378, Bartolomeo Prignano was elected Pope Urban VI. Urban's election was controversial; French cardinals claimed he was elected under duress from the Roman mob. This dispute would lead to the Western Schism when French cardinals elected an antipope in September 1378. Gregory XI's return to Rome and his death thus set in motion the events that would fracture Catholic Christendom for nearly 40 years.
key_figures: Catherine of Siena (mystic and papal advisor)

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Western Schism origins

events.certainty_note: Gregory XI's death date (27 March 1377) is documented in papal records. His return to Rome in 1377 is well-attested. The succession crisis and Urban VI's election in 1378 are clearly documented.

events.title: Hongwu Emperor establishes examination system for civil service
events.description: In 1377, the Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang) formally established or reformed the civil service examination system (keju) as a means of selecting officials based on merit rather than aristocratic birth or patronage. This system, which had roots in earlier Chinese dynasties, was revived and systematized under the Ming. The examination system was based on Confucian classics and tested candidates' knowledge of orthodox Confucian thought. This system would become a defining feature of Ming and Qing governance, creating a meritocratic bureaucracy (at least in theory) and reducing the power of hereditary aristocracy. The examinations also served as a tool for ideological control, ensuring that officials were versed in Confucian orthodoxy as interpreted by the emperor.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
Official Ming court records and examination regulations
Historiographic consensus on Ming civil service

events.certainty_note: The Ming Shilu documents the establishment or reform of the examination system, though the exact date varies by source. Some sources place the formal establishment in 1377, others in 1380 or later. The broad outline is secure, but the precise chronology is debated.

events.title: Timur defeats Husayn and consolidates power in Transoxiana
events.description: In 1377, Timur (Tamerlane) defeated his rival Husayn in a decisive battle and consolidated his control over Transoxiana (the region between the Oxus and Jaxartes rivers). This victory marked a turning point in Timur's rise to power; by 1377, he had eliminated most of his rivals and established himself as the dominant military power in Central Asia. Timur's consolidation of power in Transoxiana provided a base for his later campaigns of conquest that would eventually extend his empire from Anatolia to India. The victory over Husayn is celebrated in later Persian chronicles as a pivotal moment in Timur's career.
events.certainty_note: Timur's defeat of Husayn is attested in later Persian chronicles, but contemporary documentation is sparse. The date of 1377 is given in some sources, but others place it in 1376 or 1378. Modern scholarship generally accepts that Timur consolidated power over Transoxiana in this period, but the exact chronology is debated.

events.title: Ottoman forces capture Gallipoli from Byzantine Empire
events.description: In 1377, Ottoman forces under Murad I captured the fortress of Gallipoli (Gelibolu) from the Byzantine Empire. This was a significant strategic victory, as Gallipoli controlled the entrance to the Sea of Marmara and was a key Byzantine stronghold. The capture of Gallipoli marked a major step in Ottoman expansion into the Balkans and the decline of Byzantine power in the region. The fortress would remain under Ottoman control and become an important base for further Ottoman expansion into Europe. The loss of Gallipoli was a symbolic and strategic blow to the already weakened Byzantine Empire.
events.certainty_note: The capture of Gallipoli is attested in Ottoman and Byzantine sources, though the exact date is sometimes given as 1376 or 1377. Modern scholarship generally accepts 1377 as the date, but some sources place it earlier. The strategic importance of the capture is clear, but contemporary documentation is limited.

disconfirming_evidence: The capture of Gallipoli is sometimes dated to 1376 rather than 1377, and some sources suggest it was captured by Süleyman Pasha (Murad I's son) rather than Murad I directly. The examination system's establishment date is debated; some sources place it in 1380 or later rather than 1377. Timur's defeat of Husayn is celebrated in later chronicles, but contemporary documentation is sparse, and the exact date and significance are debated. Gregory XI's return to Rome is sometimes portrayed as a triumphant restoration of papal authority, but recent scholarship emphasizes the fragility of papal power and the political maneuvering that preceded his return.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1377 is uneven. European events (Edward III's death, Gregory XI's death, papal succession) are well-recorded in contemporary chronicles and official records. Chinese sources are detailed but compiled later and reflect imperial ideology. Ottoman and Byzantine events are documented in later chronicles with significant gaps in contemporary sources. Central Asian events rely on later Persian chronicles with sparse contemporary documentation. Sub-Saharan African, Southeast Asian, and Pacific events are virtually absent from surviving written records. The surviving sources are heavily biased toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history is underrepresented. Court-sponsored chronicles (especially Chinese and Ottoman) may exaggerate the power and achievements of their patrons. The Western Schism's origins are interpreted differently by Catholic, Orthodox, and secular historians, reflecting theological and political biases.

graph_edges.note: Edward III's death ended his 50-year reign that began in 1327
Richard II's accession and minority led to the Peasants' Revolt in 1381
Gregory XI's return to Rome ended the Avignon Papacy period
Gregory XI's death precipitated the disputed election of Urban VI
The examination system's establishment was part of Hongwu's administrative reforms
Timur's defeat of Husayn was a key step in his consolidation of power
The capture of Gallipoli was part of Murad I's expansion into the Balkans
